confidence_tiers.A: cited by 2+ named food writers from different publishers
confidence_tiers.B: cited by 1 named food writer, strong quote, canonical neighbourhood
confidence_tiers.C: auto-rejected: null neighbourhood + single source, concentration cap, or failed quality checks

sources.article_title: Local's Lisbon Restaurant Guide (2026): 42 Best Places To Eat
sources.writer_profile: Daniela Sunde-Brown is an Australian food and travel writer who has lived in Lisbon since 2018. Her independent website Ola Daniela focuses on where locals eat in the city, with a particular interest in traditional tascas and neighbourhood restaurants that avoid the tourist circuit.

Rita Geraldes is a food writer covering Lisbon for The Infatuation, the New York-based restaurant guide known for its opinionated, non-advertorial reviews. Her Lisbon guide emphasises restaurants across price points with a focus on what the experience actually feels like rather than prestige alone.

Austin Bush is a food and travel writer and photographer who contributes to Culinary Backstreets, covering neighbourhood food scenes in Lisbon. He reports on traditional and emerging restaurants for CB's annual Best Bites roundup.

sources.article_title: Chef Louise Bourrat deixa o Boubou's e abre novo restaurante em Lisboa
sources.writer_profile: Miguel Pires é o fundador e editor do Mesa Marcada, o mais influente blogue independente de gastronomia em Portugal desde 2009. Baseado em Lisboa, é também crítico gastronómico do Público e organizador dos Prémios Mesa Marcada, os prémios de restauração mais seguidos em Portugal.

sources.article_title: Prado, Lisboa: cozinha honesta com produto de pequenos produtores
sources.writer_profile: Catarina Moura é crítica gastronómica do Guia Repsol Portugal, guia de qualidade que avalia restaurantes com base em visitas anónimas. O Guia Repsol é a referência ibérica independente de gastronomia, classificado como secundário por ser publicação nacional/ibérica.

sources.article_title: Canalha: o bistro em Belém que deu nova vida à cozinha portuguesa contemporânea
sources.commercial_conflict_note: ⚠ coi: Time Out opera o Time Out Market Lisboa no Mercado da Ribeira — conflito de interesses comercial declarado. Incluído com transparência.
sources.writer_profile: Alfredo Lacerda é crítico gastronómico e editor de restaurantes da Time Out Lisboa. ⚠ coi: a Time Out opera o Time Out Market Lisboa no Mercado da Ribeira — conflito de interesses comercial declarado.

restaurants.name: Canalha
restaurants.quote: Phenomenal shareable plates without any pretentiousness — smoked eel, two-year-aged tuna tiradito, and the prato do dia make Canalha in Belém the restaurant Lisbon needed.
restaurants.notes: PT pool contributed by src-timeout-lacerda (⚠ coi). Chef João Rodrigues left Feitoria (1★) to open this market-driven bistro. Article URLs need verification before publication.

restaurants.name: Boubou's
restaurants.quote: Chef Louise Bourrat — winner of French TV show Top Chef — brought off-beat fine dining to Príncipe Real with a tasting menu that blends French precision with Lisbon irreverence.
restaurants.notes: Chef Louise Bourrat departed April 2026 and opened Gancho in Alfama (Mesa Marcada, 2026-04-08). Restaurant continues under new direction — verify current concept before publication.

restaurants.name: Prado
dishes_mentioned: smoked eel with caramelised onion rice
restaurants.signature_dish: cockles with spinach and coriander; beef tartare with dried cabbage
restaurants.quote: Farm-to-table pioneer in Lisbon's Sé district — beef tartare with dried cabbage, smoked eel with caramelised onion rice, and mushroom ice cream on a menu built around small producers.
restaurants.notes: Chef António Galapito. Located near the Sé cathedral; neighbourhood assigned Alfama per Repsol source. Some sources describe as Mouraria — boundary is ambiguous at this location.

restaurants.name: O Velho Eurico
restaurants.quote: One of Lisbon's hardest reservations — chef Zé Paulo Rocha opened this neo-tasca in Mouraria in 2019 with a slate menu of octopus lagareiro, piglet empanada, and leite-creme that became the template for a generation of Lisbon restaurants.

restaurants.name: Cervejaria Ramiro
restaurants.signature_dish: gambas à guilho; santola (spider crab); prego do lombo
restaurants.quote: Most important seafood restaurant in the city — stuffed crab, gambas à guilho, perfectly-cooked prawns, and their famous prego do lombo steak sandwich make this Intendente institution unmissable.
restaurants.notes: Operating since the 1950s. Expect queues of 30–90 minutes.

restaurants.name: Bar Alimentar
dishes_mentioned: whipped cod with caramelised onion cannoli
restaurants.signature_dish: whipped cod with caramelised onion cannoli
restaurants.quote: Feels like an after-party at the house of a minimalist artist — whipped cod with caramelised onion cannoli and fava beans vignarola in a Príncipe Real space that has been one of the city's best openings.

restaurants.name: Ultimo Porto
restaurants.quote: Best grilled fish in Lisbon — an unusual waterfront restaurant jammed up against the docks in Santos, where chocos trinchados, red mullet, and the tomato-and-onion salad are reason enough to make the trip.

restaurants.name: Taberna do Mar
restaurants.quote: Grilled sardine nigiri is the signature dish of this Graça spot that mixes Japanese and Portuguese cuisines — one of the most original combinations in Lisbon, with a short rotating menu.

restaurants.name: Pigmeu
restaurants.signature_dish: Alentejo pork preparations; liver on toast
restaurants.quote: Nose-to-tail pig concept in Campo de Ourique — the kitchen orders half a pig weekly and uses every part, with a Bib Gourmand from Michelin and a wine list of naturals to match.
restaurants.notes: Chef-owner Miguel Peres. Michelin Bib Gourmand.

restaurants.name: ZunZum Gastrobar
dishes_mentioned: filhós with cockles in garlicky sauce
restaurants.signature_dish: shellfish rice; filhós with cockles in garlicky sauce
restaurants.quote: Chef Marlene Vieira — former MasterChef Portugal judge — runs this relaxed fine dining restaurant near the cruise terminal, with shellfish rice, filhós with cockles in garlicky sauce, and a famous francesinha.

restaurants.name: Vibe
restaurants.quote: Chef Mattia Stanchieri's Michelin-recommended restaurant in Chiado — no fixed menu, customers choose by course count (5 or 7), with a Thai-influenced chapter that signals an ambition beyond borders.

restaurants.name: Taberna Albricoque
restaurants.quote: Algarve-inspired cockle pastries and a short menu of southern Portuguese dishes served in a warm, informal setting near Santa Apolónia station — a neighbourhood restaurant that has quietly become a must.

restaurants.name: Rosetta
restaurants.signature_dish: grilled peaches with burrata; clams with beurre blanc
restaurants.quote: Tiny produce-driven share-plate restaurant in Bairro Alto with grilled peaches with burrata, clams with beurre blanc, and a daily menu that changes with the market.

restaurants.name: O Frade
restaurants.quote: Michelin Bib Gourmand in Belém, known for its arrozes — rice dishes cooked to order using ingredients from small producers, with a focused wine list and a relaxed atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Taberna da Rua das Flores
restaurants.quote: Modern tavern movement pioneer in Chiado — chef André Magalhães opened this small, celebrated spot that launched a generation of Lisbon neo-tascas, with top-quality local produce and a short daily menu.

restaurants.name: Obra
restaurants.quote: Prawn brioche — a sandwich stuffed with creamy camarão — is the dish everyone orders first at this Santos spot, where the kitchen keeps things simple and ingredient-led.

restaurants.name: Corrupio
restaurants.quote: Central bar, excellent octopus salad, and a rotating menu of small plates at Cais do Sodré — casual enough to drop in without a booking, serious enough to make you stay longer than planned.

restaurants.name: Taberna Sal Grosso
restaurants.quote: Tuna and fried quail are among the specialties at this tightly packed Alfama cubby hole — chalkboard menu, excellent natural wines, and prices that still feel fair despite the fame.

restaurants.name: Hachi Kare-Ya
restaurants.quote: Japanese curry specialist near Anjos station — a single-dish concept that has quietly become one of the most talked-about cheap eats in Lisbon's Arroios neighbourhood.

restaurants.name: SEM
restaurants.signature_dish: razor clam escabeche; honey-roasted carrots with pomegranate
restaurants.quote: Seven-course tasting menu with a zero-waste ethos — honey-roasted carrots with pomegranate, razor clam escabeche, and sophisticated delicate cooking from former alumni of Silo in London.
restaurants.notes: Mesa Marcada (April 2026) reported an event described as among SEM's last nights. Open status requires verification before publication — restaurant may have closed.

restaurants.name: Pap'Açôrda
restaurants.quote: Synonymous with Lisbon nightlife — peixinhos da horta, ameijoas à bulhão pato, and açorda at Cais do Sodré, in a restaurant that has managed to stay genuinely good despite decades of fame.

restaurants.name: O Maravilhas
restaurants.quote: Traditional Portuguese spot in Alcântara with big portions — scrambled eggs with farinheira, cod fritters, fried green beans, and serradura dessert at prices that feel like a throwback.

restaurants.name: Tasca Pete
restaurants.quote: Feels a bit like going to a friend's place for dinner — blueberry focaccia, potato terrine, and a short daily menu that Pete changes based on what arrived from the market that morning.

restaurants.name: Essencial
restaurants.signature_dish: oven-baked rice with scarlet shrimp; turbot with caviar
restaurants.quote: Feels more like having dinner inside someone's house in Bairro Alto — oven-baked rice with scarlet shrimp, turbot with caviar, and pigeon foie gras in an intimate 30-seat room with no sign outside.
